sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is both diagnostic and therapeutic. The primary goals are: 1) to obtain sufficient tissue for accurate histopathologic and molecular diagnosis, 2) to achieve maximal safe resection to improve survival and local control, 3) to relieve mass effect and symptoms (e.g., seizures, increased intracranial pressure), and 4) to reduce tumor burden to enhance the efficacy of adjuvant therapies [BRAIN-B].

Principles:
- Maximal safe resection is recommended whenever feasible, guided by preoperative MRI and intraoperative adjuncts (neuronavigation, intraoperative MRI, awake mapping, fluorescence-guided surgery) [BRAIN-B].
- For diffuse gliomas, supramarginal resection (including T2/FLAIR abnormalities) is suggested for IDH-mutant tumors [BRAIN-B footnote 1].
- For ependymoma, gross total resection is a strong prognostic factor; re-resection should be considered if initial resection is incomplete [EPEN-1].
- For PCNSL, stereotactic biopsy is preferred over resection, as maximal resection does not improve survival [PCNS-1].
- Postoperative MRI within 48 hours (brain) and 2-3 weeks (spine) is required to assess extent of resection [BRAIN-B].
- Tissue should be reviewed by an experienced neuropathologist with molecular testing [BRAIN-E].

Resectability criteria: Resectability depends on tumor location, proximity to eloquent cortex, patient performance status, and expected benefit. Factors include: 1) Age and PS, 2) Tumor pathology (benign vs. malignant), 3) Feasibility of decreasing mass effect, 4) Number and location of lesions, 5) Time since last surgery for recurrent disease [BRAIN-B]. For NF2-SWN, resectability of vestibular schwannoma depends on size, hearing status, and risk of bilateral hearing loss [BRAIN-G].

Pre-operative optimisation: Optimize performance status with corticosteroids for edema. Antiepileptic drugs may be used perioperatively if seizure history. Prophylactic antibiotics should be considered. Consult neuroanesthesia and neurocritical care as needed. For spinal metastases, preoperative embolization may be considered for vascular tumors (e.g., renal cell carcinoma) [BRAIN-B, SPINE-2].

cards.name: Gross total resection (GTR)
cards.detail:
Approach: Craniotomy with microsurgical technique
Indication: Primary goal for most primary brain tumors (gliomas, ependymomas, metastatic lesions) when safe [BRAIN-B].
Key complications: Infection, hemorrhage, new neurological deficits (e.g., motor, speech), seizures [BRAIN-B].
Operative mortality: Generally low (<1-2%) in specialized centers [BRAIN-B].

cards.name: Stereotactic biopsy
cards.detail:
Approach: Frameless or frame-based stereotactic guidance
Indication: Deep-seated or inoperable lesions, or when diagnosis is uncertain [BRAIN-B].
Key complications: Hemorrhage, infection, sampling error [BRAIN-B].
Operative mortality: <1% [BRAIN-B].

cards.name: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT)
cards.detail:
Approach: MRI-guided ablation
Indication: Poor surgical candidates with relapsed brain metastases, radiation necrosis, or recurrent gliomas (category 2B) [BRAIN-B].
Key complications: Edema, infection, hemorrhage [BRAIN-B].
Operative mortality: Low [BRAIN-B].

cards.name: Carmustine wafer implantation
cards.detail:
Approach: Placement in resection cavity during surgery
Indication: High-grade glioma resection (category 2B). Note: May impact clinical trial eligibility [GLIO-9].
Key complications: Increased risk of seizures and infection [GLIO-9].
Operative mortality: Minimal added risk [GLIO-9].
